title: Soft Start for 'Abigail' Another Scary Sign for Horror at Box Office
text: Abigail's soft opening weekend of $10.2 million raises questions about the health of the horror genre at the box office. Despite strong critical reviews (82% on Rotten Tomatoes) and positive festival reactions, the film underperformed industry expectations by $2-5 million. However, some industry observers argue that not all horror underperformance should be attributed to a genre-wide crisis. Horror films have historically been reliable revenue generators, with productions generally operating on smaller budgets than mainstream action or comedy films. The key differentiator appears to be originality and freshness within the genre. Films like M3GAN succeeded despite a crowded market, suggesting that audiences respond to genuinely novel concepts and strong execution. Abigail's performance as part of a saturated April release calendar—with seven horror films in eight weeks—may have contributed to its underperformance. Marketing factors also played a role, as some audiences may not have been aware of the film's unique premise of a young vampire ballerina. The film's B CinemaScore suggests lukewarm audience enthusiasm despite critical acclaim, indicating a potential mismatch between critical and commercial reception in the horror space.
